The Von Neumann architecture, also known as the "Fortran architecture", is a family of architecture-based computer programming languages first coined in 1965 by Robert A. von Neumann.  The von Neumann architecture was designed to handle the codebreaking tasks of the microprocessor era, and was employed throughout the development of modern CPUs such as the Intel X86 and the AMD EPYC architecture. As of 2022, 64-bit CPUs are standard across most CPUs architectures, and 64-bit architectures have surpassed 32-bit CPUs as the leading architecture for data and compute workloads.
